books.title: Exhalation
books.author: Ted Chiang
books.review:
This is a stunning collection of short stories, remarkably not only for its philosophical depth blended with science fiction, but also for the elegance of its prose.

Chiang expertly explores themes of fate, free will, time, and consciousness with both precision and emotional weight, making each piece feel thought-provoking in its own distinct right. One of the things that struck me, especially in the first story, is how seamlessly his writing adapts to the voice of each narrator. Whether capturing the lyrical cadence of a fable or the precision of a scientist’s observations, Chiang’s writing style enhances the immersion and emotional resonance of each piece.

While not every story resonated with me personally, the sheer range of ideas explored — from free will and time travel to consciousness and AI — is astonishing. Thoughtful, beautifully written, and intellectually rich, Exhalation is a collection that lingers long after reading.

—
I read this as part of a work book club, and while the structured pace of these groups often dampens my overall immersion in a story, Exhalation proved to be an exceptionally fitting choice—especially for a book club of software engineers (lol). The collection’s intricate explorations of logic, determinism, and technology felt particularly relevant, made even more interesting by Chiang’s own background as a computer science major.

books.title: Out of the Gobi: My Story of China and America
books.author: Weijian Shan
books.review:
This was an utterly captivating read for me, but pinpointing just one reason why is tricky. Maybe it’s the way Weijian Shan effortlessly intertwines the Cultural Revolution, international business, academia, and his relentless pursuit of success across continents. Or maybe it’s the way his story makes me feel wildly unproductive by comparison.... In any case, his memoir strikes an excellent balance between a deeply personal account of surviving Mao's Cultural Revolution & a broader exploration of how sheer perseverance can lead one to navigate the complexities of global capitalism and academia.

Before this, my understanding of the Cultural Revolution was as fragmented as most people’s—sporadic lessons in school (very few), passive comments from my parents, a couple of documentaries, and the occasional Wikipedia rabbit hole. My parents' generation narrowly missed being caught in the upheaval—my mom’s older sister was just a year or two off from the students affected. That made Shan’s perspective particularly valuable, especially since it’s not just a firsthand account, but a nuanced, English-language narrative (with a slight Western lens) that breaks through China’s Great Firewall, even if today's modern China has a more critical impression of Mao. His story stands out in a world where firsthand, unfiltered perspectives on this period, written in English, are scarce. Shan’s account feels like finally hearing the full, unvarnished tale from someone who lived it—and there’s something uniquely compelling about hearing about academia, business, and history from a man who also had to shovel dirt in the Gobi Desert. It’s the minor details, too, that linger with me. Decades later, memory naturally falters, but Shan writes with such vivid clarity that I couldn’t help but wonder: how much of this is drawn from painstakingly kept journals, and how much has been shaped by the passage of time? In any case, it is remarkably impressive.

What makes the book even better is that Shan doesn’t chronicle his trials with a grim sense of duty. His writing is sharp, witty, and—dare I say—entertaining. His reflections on resilience, determination, and the pursuit of dreams across cultural and political divides left me not just thinking about China’s past, but also about how history shapes individuals in ways we rarely consider. While reading, I mentioned this book to an older coworker whose parents were laborers during the same generation. He pointed me toward learning more about the “alumni” of the Red Guard—like Song Binbin, who recently passed away and whose complex legacy, including earning a doctorate at MIT after beating eight teachers to death, raises difficult ethical questions. To what extent can someone be forgiven for their role in a turbulent period like the Cultural Revolution, when their later life contributes positively to society?

In short, Out of the Gobi is more than just a memoir of hardship and survival—it’s a deftly written, slyly humorous narrative of global ambition and political navigation. Plus, it leaves you feeling intellectually enriched and maybe just a little inadequate at the same time.

books.title: Caste: The Origins of Our Discontents
books.author: Isabel Wilkerson
books.review:
Harrowing yet profoundly impactful read. Wilkerson does an incredible job of weaving history, personal anecdotes, and systematic analysis to illuminate connections between caste systems in the US, India, and Nazi Germany. I found the comparisons striking and deeply unsettling, particularly the revelation of how U.S. racial policies influenced Nazi Germany's racial laws—an insight that left me both horrified and reflective.
I really admired the author’s writing style and her skillful use of metaphors to create a lyrical and deeply resonant narrative. For instance, the metaphor of an old house—deceptively stable yet fraught with hidden dangers—offers a striking lens to view the undercurrents of systemic inequality in America. The book seamlessly weaves together the past and present, moving fluidly from the history of slavery and segregation to contemporary examples of racial injustice. This seamless transition underscores how deeply the past informs the present, making the book both timeless and profoundly relevant.
While I didn’t agree with everything, particularly some of the generalizations, I still found the book exemplary in challenging my perspective and forcing me to confront uncomfortable truths. It captures how America can simultaneously feel like a land of opportunity and a land of terror for many—a duality that’s feels both sobering and urgent to address.

books.title: When Breath Becomes Air
books.author: Paul Kalanithi
books.review:
Breathtaking and deeply moving – this is easily my favourite book this year so far. This book emotionally broke me; I found myself fully engrossed in it and unable to put it down despite final projects (lol rip compilers). Even after I finished it, for the rest of that day, and many days afterward, I found myself reflecting on the messages within, hesitant to pick up another book knowing it couldn't compare to how this book made me feel (… and then finals hit).

Paul Kalanithi's prose is sophisticated, elegant, and seamless, revealing his extensive literary background. This book is far more than the story of a gifted neurosurgeon confronting terminal cancer at the height of his career; it is a profound exploration of life, death, and the essence of living. As someone who has recently been contemplating life’s fragility and the meaning of life, this memoir struck a deep chord within me, especially because it reminded me of a very close friend who lost their battle with cancer. Reading Kalanithi's journey led me to reminisce on our cherished shared memories and conversations, as well as the valuable lessons he taught me about life, resilience, and purpose.

Kalanithi's religious and philosophical reflections resonated with my own Christian beliefs, and I really enjoyed reading about his evolving relationship with faith – from his departure to his eventual return – and throughout it, how he wove together his knowledge of medicine and literature to explore questions about human existence, morality, and spirituality.

This is a powerful and unforgettable memoir that serves as a poignant reminder of life's fragility and beauty, urging us to cherish those you have around you.

books.title: Girl Decoded: A Scientist's Quest to Reclaim Our Humanity by Bringing Emotional Intelligence to Technology
books.author: Rana El Kaliouby
books.review:
"I have learned that being attuned to our emotions, and being unafraid to show and act on them, is empowering. That's how you build strong personal connections versus putting up a barrier, whether in the digital or real world. It creates true empathy; we become more understanding and accepting of ourselves and of others."

An utterly compelling read, Girl Decoded traverses a vast emotional and intellectual landscape — growing up amid political upheaval, breaking barriers as a woman of color in male-dominated industries, enduring the pain of divorce and single motherhood while an ocean away from family, and confronting profound grief and loss. It also wrestles with the challenge of embracing emotional honesty in a world that often discourages vulnerability. I especially appreciated her candid reflections on faith and how it shaped her values and ambitions.

The first part of the book was particularly striking — her storytelling was candid and vulnerable, making her journey all the more engaging. The way she interwove her personal struggles with the evolution of her groundbreaking research gave her insights on Affectiva and AI a depth that made them feel especially compelling. That said, the latter portion of the book took on a more promotional tone, focusing heavily on her startup and technology. While this is undoubtedly significant, and normally right up my alley, it pulled me out of the immersive experience of reading a memoir.

Still, I found this to be a standout. In a genre where business and career memoirs are overwhelmingly written by white men, it was both refreshing and powerful to read the story of a woman of color who not only navigated but thrived in spaces where she was often the only one.

Remarkably Bright Creatures is a sweet and charming novel with a unique premise that sets it apart from typical contemporary fiction exploring aging and grief. At its heart is Marcellus, a curmudgeonly yet endearing octopus whose sharp observations bring warmth and wit to the story. His perspective adds a refreshing and whimsical touch that makes the novel truly stand out.

While I found it an enjoyable and refreshing read, especially in contrast to the heavier titles I usually pick up, I felt that many of the characters, particularly the male ones, lacked depth. They weren’t especially compelling or likable, which made parts of the story less engaging. Still, the novel’s warmth, originality, and heartfelt themes make it a very worthwhile read, even if not all of the story elements fully resonated with me.

books.title: A Thousand Brains: A New Theory of Intelligence
books.author: Jeff Hawkins
books.review:
A Thousand Brains is a mixed bag, but there’s no denying that Jeff Hawkins is brilliant, and that brilliance shines in the first half of the book. His “thousand brains” model of intelligence — where countless small models work together within the neocortex — offers an elegant, intuitive framework for understanding cognition. The theory is ambitious yet accessible for the lay person, striking a balance between neuroscience and computational thinking. Even though it’s light on technical details, the way Hawkins distills complex ideas into a coherent and engaging narrative is impressive.

But then the second half of the book happens. Hawkins shifts from theory to speculation, offering a broad and underwhelming discussion on AI, consciousness, and the future of intelligence. Much of it feels like well-worn territory, covered in greater depth and nuance elsewhere. While the book starts strong, I felt that the latter half lacks the same rigor, leaving little lasting impression.

I’d recommend the first half to anyone curious about computational neuroscience and intelligence, but the second half? You can probably skip it.

books.title: First They Killed My Father: A Daughter of Cambodia Remembers
books.author: Loung Ung
books.review:
I picked this up very spontaneously (woohoo, what a cheery start to 2025!) —I was on a passive streak of reading about communism in different eras and region, and the title caught my eye.
This was a tough read. The first chapter was so cheery and joyful that I knew I was going to feel crushed by the time the titular moment arrived.
There's something particularly striking about reading a child's voice, retroactively infused with the cognitive awareness of an adult. Even though the narrator is between five and eight years old, the past-tense reflection adds layers of imagination, coping, processing, and depth that I really appreciated. I can understand critiques about the book's legitimacy or accuracy as a historical source, but honestly, so many moments gave me such intense chills or made me cry. I would read this before going to bed; I read this before going to work, and I think by the time I put it down to carry on with my night or day, I really felt awash with the realization that we in the Western world have so much to be grateful for.
Would absolutely recommend this for an "easy", albeit not light, read.

books.title: The Reason for God: Belief in an Age of Skepticism
books.author: Timothy J. Keller
books.review:
Over the holiday season, I felt compelled to pick up this book by Timothy Keller. I've had it on my shelf since elementary school, but never finished it cover to cover. But this time, being back home after living in NYC for over a year, I found myself reflecting on Redeemer and my coincidental proximity to the church branch now led by his son. I also wanted to seek a more accessible and modern take on apologetics—something less academic but still intellectually engaging.
Keller does an excellent job of presenting a theologically sound yet approachable defense of Christianity. I found his writing inviting rather than confrontational, fostering genuine dialogue between skeptics and believers. He anticipates common objections to faith and responds with reasoned arguments, drawing from philosophy, literature, and personal experience to make his case.
Some of the earlier chapters, particularly those addressing skepticism, were compelling, though I felt they could have been strengthened with more theological depth. At times, the book seemed caught between being an introductory read for the layperson and a more rigorous exploration of apologetics. Despite this, I found The Reason for God to be an insightful and accessible work—one that felt particularly relevant and thought-provoking for me at this moment.
Would absolutely recommend this to anyone curious about faith and who generally enjoys non-fiction. It’s a compelling and engaging read that presents complex ideas in a way that is both stimulating and approachable.

books.title: The Design of Everyday Things
books.author: Donald A. Norman
books.review:
This is a great read for anyone even remotely curious about design. It’s surprisingly fun and witty—honestly quite the achievement for a book that's standard assigned reading in many college classes. You’d expect a textbook vibe, but instead, you get Norman cracking jokes (though, how good they are is an exercise left to the reader), making you reconsider whether you're using a door correctly. Each chapter also feels like its own little lesson, so you can really jump in wherever and still get a lot out of it.

A few parts had me grinning, like his take on metro systems, especially the Paris Metro. Having lived in France for a couple of years, I can confirm the chaos coming from a North American perspective, and quirks he mentions are very real—and yes, still kind of endearing in hindsight.

I also looove when what I'm reading connects to other things I’ve read or ideas I’ve recently come across. Towards the end, Norman dives into chess and references Erik Brynjolfsson and Andrew McAfee’s thoughts on the evolution of human and technological collaboration, referencing chess, including the Garry Kasparov vs. Deep Blue match. Funny coincidence: Erik was the PhD advisor of a professor I emailed just last week, and I’d actually read one of their books last year for a class—and in it, they discussed Kasparov and chess. Then, two days ago, Kasparov himself was brought in to my workplace for a special talk. It felt like such a wild, full-circle moment.

Overall, DOET is a solid introduction to how we interact with the world, with a good handful of clever insights and the occasional "Wait, what?" moment to keep you on your toes.

books.title: Mindset: The New Psychology of Success
books.author: Carol S. Dweck
books.review:
the core idea —having a growth mindset versus fixed mindset— may seem almost too simple, but the examples given throughout the book really brings the idea to life. i think it can be easy to forget to what extent simple theories can form the basis of a lot of our understanding of the world. the book does a good job at breaking down how adopting a growth mindset can improve everything from work, to relationships, to learning new skills.
that said, it did feel kind of repetitive and stretched out, as if a solid idea was padded out a bit too much. while the message was very useful, the repetition and some of the simplicity of the message kind of wore me down. at a lot of points, the cynic in me felt like the "mindset" framework just felt like an easy, blanket statement to explain everything wrong in life.
while it's not always that simple, i think the message, coupled with the context of maybe not just using it as an umbrella to apply to everything, is very solid and was a good reminder. usually the simplicity & borderline ...triteness?? of the book would rub me a bit more of the wrong way, but i more refreshed than i expected reading this one.

books.review:
this is cute! aj jacobs takes readers on an interesting, lighthearted journey in thanks a thousand, where he sets out to thank every single person involved in making his morning cup of joe. the premise is charming, refreshing, and novel, and jacob's wry humor and curiosity shines through. it's a delightful and quaint reminder of the many people we take for granted in our daily lives.
however, while the concept is compelling, i think the ted talk format works better, and a condensed version packs a stronger punch. imo, the ted talk captures the essence of the message in a more succinct & engaging manner. in congrats, the book provides a deeper dive into the topic, but felt unnecessarily drawn out in parts, which kind of took away from the impact.
overall: a cute, feel-good read wrapped up with witty and self-deprecating humor, but the message comes across better in the ted talk format. solidly written so if you're a fan of his work, still worth picking up, but for a quick dose of gratitude and perspective, i think the talk is more effective.

Jacobs discovers that his coffee—and every other item in our lives—would not be possible without hundreds of people we usually take for granted: farm…

books.title: The Women
books.author: Kristin Hannah
books.review:
the women was a refreshing (albeit quite dark) read, largely due to its unique theme and deeply flawed main character, who felt authentic and complex in ways i don't always find in historical fiction. the pacing was well-balanced, keeping the story engaging without dragging, and the focus on a woman's role during the vietnam war set it apart from other novels i've read. the novel powerfully captures how women, especially in a war so widely despised, were vilified and treated as outsiders, forced to navigate both the trauma of the battlefield and the double-edged stigma from fellow veterans and compatriots upon returning home.
these were themes i hadn’t previously considered deeply, despite having read extensively on feminism, the political and historical implications of the vietnam war, nixon era, and watergate scandal — i always appreciate reading anything that challenges me to think about a new perspective; the kinds of themes that make you stop and think "dang, i hadn't thought about that before".
ultimately, it’s a story of personal growth, resilience, and the struggle for women to reclaim their identity in a society eager to forget their contributions.

But in 1965, the world is changing, and she suddenly dares to imagine a different future for herself. books.title: Burn Rate: Launching a Startup and Losing My Mind
books.author: Andy     Dunn
books.review:
a strikingly candid memoir that offers a fresh and, in my opinion, much-needed perspective on the intersection of mental illness and entrepreneurship. his writing is excellent: the pacing pulls in the reader effortlessly, while his clarity and emotional depth make the story resonate on multiple levels. this isn't your typical business or success narrative; it's a story about the often-unspoken challenges that come with high-stakes ambition.

dunn's exploration of bipolar disorder, which is set against the backdrop of building a startup, provides a new look into the pressures of perceived success. his vulnerability in sharing his mental health struggles adds layers to what could otherwise have been just another tale (albeit very compelling) of the entrepreneurial hustle and 'grindset'. instead, this sheds light on a side of success that's rarely told, making for a very refreshing read for anyone navigating the complexities of mental health x high pressure environments x professional achievement.

that said, i felt like the sheer honesty was simultaneously the biggest strength and weakness of this book -- it should absolutely be acknowledged how remarkably privileged & lucky he was to have made it through some of the things that happened to him (and without being penalized for some of the things he did), but i think this just goes to show how few people can live to tell such a compelling tale as this one, and that the flaw here doesn't lie with him, but with the system itself.

this is a both a business book and a deeply human story that challenges the notion that success and well-being are always aligned, making it a compelling read both from the perspective of someone interested in both entrepreneurship and (mental) health.

books.title: Prisoners of Geography: Ten Maps That Tell You Everything You Need to Know About Global Politics (Politics of Place, #1)
books.author: Tim  Marshall
books.review:
this is a solid introduction to geopolitics, particularly if you don’t have very much familiarity with the subject. the book explores how geography shapes the political and economic realities of various regions around the world, with each chapter focussing on a different geographical area, diving into how natural borders, resources, and terrain have influenced the strategies and struggles of nations (rivers, rivers, rIVERS).

while the book covered a broad range of topics and provided a solid foundation for understanding the geopolitical landscape, it did so in a way that felt kind of disjointed. the chapters felt kind of loosely connected, lacking a clear narrative thread or ordering that guides the reader from one region to the next. as a result, the transitions between chapters felt kind of random and abrupt, making the overall structure somewhat unclear? like reading a bunch of independent articles — which is fine, but felt less compelling than it could have been. another concern i had was with the author's apparent bias, which at times borders on being discriminatory. Marshall’s perspective often reflects an anglosaxon western-centric neoliberal worldview, and his portrayals of certain regions and cultures can come across as overly simplistic, if not outright dismissive. this detracts from the objectivity one might expect from a work on geopolitics and could alienate readers who are looking for a more balanced and nuanced analysis.

i think this also lacked the depth and engaging storytelling that could captivate readings with prior knowledges of the subject. the content is more descriptive than analytical, and the writing, although accessible, doesn’t always bring the complexities of geopolitics to life in a compelling way.

overall — a very useful primer for those looking to get a basic understanding of how geography influences global politics, but definitely a bit biased and not as engaging or insightful for readers who are already reasonably well-informed on the topic.

i think this would be a good recommendation for someone looking for an easy escape into a bookish world in japan (my dream fr), something that feels light, but a is ultimately a book that lacks the depth to leave a lasting impression.

books.title: Stay True
books.author: Hua Hsu
books.review:
“what we considered real life was just a state of permanent dreaming… i remember thinking, given the choice, would i choose reality or the dulcet ignorance of a dream?”

this was a truly poignant exploration of friendship, identity, and grief. the memoir resonated deeply with me at times, especially as it made me reflect on the passing of one of my close college friends from cancer. hsu's introspective journey through his own loss prompted me to grapple with similar questions about the depth of human connections and the identity crises that can follow the death of someone close. namely – his reflections on the nature of memory are ones i think about sometimes, like how revisiting memories make them less accurate and fade – “maybe i misremembered a lot along the way. or a little thing was replayed in my mind so often that it hardened into the memory of having once been routine.” could definitely relate very viscerally to the struggles of remembering and cherish the details of lives taken too soon. his portrayal of grief, though at times fleeting and introspective, is crafted with a prosaic yet raw beauty.

hsu’s narrative uniquely examines the asian american experience, particularly through the lens of male + asian friendship – a topic rarely i feel is explored in literature. i especially appreciated his raw honesty, like talking about going therapy, given how therapy is often taboo in asian households. the way he described being more adept with language than our parents, as a skill we could use against them, adds a nuanced layer to the discussion of cultural identity as children of immigrants, especially since he became a writer / pursued the non-technical domains that many of our immigrant parents pursue.

i did feel very lost on the detail of his life at the beginning of the book – it made the initial chapters feel very slow and i felt like a lot of this word-real-estate could have been spent fleshing out his other friends who characterized his college days, and maybe more on their shared grief. that said, hsu’s lyrical reflections on reality, memory, and the nature of friendship became more compelling as the narrative progressed. his thoughts on measuring the significance of our grief and the meaning of friendships were also thought-provoking and a bit heart-wrenching to read. it was also interesting to read this after notes on grief by c.s. lewis earlier this year – very interesting to almost compare & contrast grief from a different perspective – e.g., a friend, as opposed to a lover; from a secular asian diaspora, as opposed to a christian perspective.

books.title: Four Thousand Weeks: Time Management for Mortals
books.author: Oliver Burkeman
books.review:
this is a different take of our limited time on earth and how we can make the most of it. having recently passed the one-year mark since moving to nyc (there go 52 weeks of my life), this book resonated deeply with me as i have been thinking about the realities of time’s finitude and whether i have or am making time for what matters.

i appreciated how burkeman challenges the traditional notions of productivity, arguing instead that the relentless pursuit of efficiency – focusing on “big rocks” that should fill jars first, instead of “small pebbles” that are easy, but matter less – often detracts from a life worth living. by starting with the “small rocks,” we risk missing out on the richness of life’s mundane yet beautiful experiences. in other words, one of his central theories is that the hyperproductivity culture can strip away the very essence of what makes life meaningful.

some other interesting ideas include: the social regulation of time, highlighting how societal pressures shape our perceptions and experiences of time. his observation that “often the unsmoothed textures of life make it livable” is a powerful reminder that the imperfections and interruptions we often strive to eliminate are what give life its depth. he also notes an intriguing phenomenon from sweden: the more people take time off work simultaneously, the happier they become.

overall, a compelling call to reconsider how we spend our fleeting time and embrace life’s imperfect beauty. it felt especially profound to read this shortly after finishing “being mortal” by atul gawande, on how modern medicine and technology interact with end of life care – especially when considering the limitations of technology, and especially in this case, process, when it comes at the expense of the quality of life.

books.title: The Rape of Nanking: The Forgotten Holocaust Of World War II
books.author: Iris Chang
books.review:
Growing up in Canada and spending many childhood summers visiting relatives in Europe, my perspective on World War II was decidedly skewed towards the Western front. I only recently thought about this bias and considered the overlooked atrocities in the East, leading me to Iris Chang's "The Rape of Nanking". This book offers a gut-wrenching examination of one of the 20th century's most horrifying and neglected atrocities. The author's tragic end lends an additional layer of haunted melancholy to the narrative.

I wanted to read this book for a while, but my decision to prioritize this book was triggered by a spontaneous trip to Hiroshima, where I visited the Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Museum – which, to its credit, was an incredibly moving experience. Despite its powerful impact (and the tears I shed as I walked through the exhibits), I could see why the museum had been criticized for being one-sided, even if it wasn’t meant to be an all-inclusive war museum. Its singular minute references to Nanking and the Potsdam Declaration were glaringly noticeable. This led me to reflect on the stark contrast in how Germany and Japan have been treated post-WWII and their respective reparations (or lack thereof). For instance, I remember my aunt and uncle pointing out Stolpersteine whenever we passed one, and how it was a crime in Germany to deny the Holocaust. Yet, despite the slaughter of hundreds of thousands of Chinese over six weeks, the Nanking Massacre is largely ignored in both Western and (especially) Japanese history – a fact that Chang illuminates with distressing clarity.

While the insufficiency of Japan's reparations is infuriating, Chang subtly underscores the Chinese government's role in this narrative, suggesting that their inaction inadvertently contributed to the massacre's faded memory. Perhaps it was due to a desire to move on, or the geopolitical climate, but the consequence remains the same – a woeful lack of justice for the victims and survivors. This adds an intriguing dimension to the book, reminding us that complicity can be broader than we think. Both Japan and China fall short in their responsibility towards victims and survivors.

Chang's vivid prose is surprisingly accessible despite the disturbing topic, balancing a range of perspectives from victims, perpetrators, and foreign observers. I went into this book thinking that it was exclusively a Chinese tragedy, but the heavy testimonies of foreigners residing in China starkly rectified this opinion. This impartial (or rather, as impartial as it could have been) narrative gives her the latitude to describe events from a more objective point of view. Her work serves as a chilling reminder of humanity's capacity for brutality – an evocation underscored by her remarkable writing talent. Despite its harrowing content, the book gives a voice to the silent victims, compelling us to reflect on our global response to atrocities.

While I wouldn't actively recommend this due to its subject matter, the introduction and two epilogues in the 2011 edition offer valuable insights into this historical event (and Chang as an individual). This powerful piece of literature illuminates a dark corner of human history. Its impact is amplified when considering the tragic demise of its bright, brilliant author.

books.title: Educated
books.author: Tara Westover
books.review:
this was such a captivating memoir to read -- it was so ethereally and clearly written, capturing the raw and vulnerable essence of Westover's journey through life. her prose is not only eloquent but also incredibly personal, making her incredibly compelling / absurd / bizarre / idiosyncratic journey through life remarkably readable. Westover's candidness in sharing her unconventional upbringing and the challenges she faced on her quest for education and gradually breaking out of her family's shell is both brave and moving. the ability for her to convey the complexities of her experiences with such clarity adds such depth to the narrative, making for very thoughtful read.
i thought that the ending (last few sentences) was also nothing short of excellent; what a satisfying end to the book! it left a lasting 'aha!' type impression, offering a sense of closure while leaving room to reflect on the power of resilience and the pursuit of knowledge.
as an aside: i thought that this book also bears a poignant resemblance to "The Glass Castle," which i read years ago. both memoirs delicately explore the transformative power of education amidst deeply (different) unconventional upbringings -- it almost felt like their upbringings were quite similar yet diametrically opposed in the most interesting ways.
ultimately: a powerful, beautifully written testament to the strength of the human spirit and the transformative power of education (and the pursuit of it). Westover's ability to convey her story with such grace and humility makes this book a must-read!

books.title: Weyward
books.author: Emilia Hart
books.review:
i really wanted to like this?? i don't pick up books expecting to give them a low rating / to dislike them, but there was quite literally not really anything about this book i liked??? i guess i found my first 1-star read...

this novel left me grappling with very conflicting emotions. i was drawn to the promise & premise of a tale centered around three women across different eras, connected to each other and nature, but as i kept reading, i found myself struggling to connect with the characters and the overall tone of the book.

while the affinity for nature of the three main characters is compelling, the pervasive theme of their suffering at the hands of men is honestly just too much. the constant portrayal of abuse and mistreatment made it really hard for me to empathize with the characters or feel invested in their plotlines.

i also personally felt like the plot was a bit incohesive? the misery of the characters overshadowed any semblance of a structured narrative (at least to me), which just made reading this book feel like a chore. i could tell the book was steering towards a hopeful/positive ending, but honestly, the vast, vast majority of it just felt so miserable.

the premise was honestly so promising and had so much potential for a richer exploration of the magic or nature (or, nature-based magic), but this just felt like a missed opportunity. i thought the heavy emphasis on the women's suffering detracted far too much from the novel's potential to deliver a more nuanced story, and as a reader, i wanted to feel captivated by the characters' journey, but instead i just felt disheartened and disconnected and the resolution really did nothing for me...

while there are some moments of poetic prose and a compelling premise, this book was just a no for me. i would have loved to read something that holistically felt more empowering, but this just left me with an immense & lingering sense of disappointment and discomfort.

books.title: The Death of Ivan Ilych
books.author: Leo Tolstoy
books.review:
this novella encapsulates the profoundness of life & death within a concise, but effective narrative.

tolstoy distills the life and ultimate demise of ivan ilych, a high-ranking russian official, who, faced with a terminal illness, confronts the emptiness of a life lived consumed by the pursuit of social status and approval. as ilych confronts his own mortality, he realizes the relative hollowness of his existence, devoid of genuine connection and purpose. i liked how the book begins at the chronological end of the story, painting the apathetic and banal discourse among Ilych's surviving acquaintances, who detachedly dissect the passing of their former peer.

the concise story cuts to the core of the human experience, exposing the superficiality of societal norms & the vacancy that accompany a life lived to fulfill social expectations. but another interesting takeaway i had was the pervasive misunderstanding of his suffering, highlighting both the challenge of empathizing with individuals in end-life-life care (and in general...), and the isolation often experienced by those nearing death.

books.title: The Man Who Solved the Market: How Jim Simons Launched the Quant Revolution
books.author: Gregory Zuckerman
books.review:
i was pretty hopeful for this one... from its premise, you would think that it's a deep dive into the genius of jim simons & his journey with rentec. but surprisingly(?), the book spends a very significant portion of its pages diving into the lives and perspectives of others. while these profiles do offer rich context, i couldn't help but feel like the title was a bit misleading. rather than detailed elucidations on market intricacies, the narration felt a bit awash with political drama & intrigue, making it feel more like a captivating recount of the 2016 republican election theatrics than a dedicated study of rentec and jim simons.
to zuckerman's credit, his examination of the political landscape is thought-provoking and i absolutely would have been interested in reading a book about the political (and by extension, ethical) drama that rentec & these quantitative firms brought about, i just didn't feel like i learned very much about rentec or jim simons that i could have gotten from reading a 5 minute article. it does shed a fascinating light on the intertwined relationship between politics and financial markets (read: extreme wealth). but if you're expecting an in-depth, (but still lay, since rentec is very secretive) exploration of simon's mathematical prowess and its application to the hedge fund industry, this book definitely isn't that.

books.title: Ripe
books.author: Sarah Rose Etter
books.review:
Ivy League grads complain about the snack selection from a conference room with a view of unhoused people bathing in the bay.

This line in the description of the book absolutely HAD me && this is the reason I pushed it up my reading list. The number of people I know who fit that line...... (ok maybe not view of the bay tho)

I liked this, and I thought it was a fun (or rather – as "fun" as it could be, given that the protagonist is miserable and seriously depressed) read and take on Silicon Valley. But my main gripe is that the timing of this book felt very reactive to a time passed by. I think Silicon Valley took a pretty heavy hit culture-wise with layoffs over the past year, and this book felt like it was dwelling on public perception of the start-up scene up until the late 2010s in not the most innovative manner. After seeing a lot of my friends join and even start their own Silicon Valley based start-ups, I felt like the stereotyping was a bit pretentious and perhaps problematic at times? (Although it would be a lie to say I didn't laugh or low-key agree with some of it!) Maybe my take is skewed because it’s largely informed by early-career “believers”, but I absolutely wouldn’t say they all are.

Had this book come out a couple of years ago, closer in time to other major exposés on Silicon Valley, I think it would have been a lot more interesting. I think knowing now what I know about the industry, some of it fell a bit flat, but it was still a page-turning, artistically-crafted read about corporate greed and workplace depression. The writing was beautiful and easily digestible, the layout of the book conceptually was unique and enjoyable!

books.title: All That Moves Us: A Pediatric Neurosurgeon, His Young Patients, and Their Stories of Grace and Resilience
books.author: Jay Wellons
books.review:
All That Moves Us is a poignant and thoughtful account of a neurosurgeon's journey through grief, drawing inspiration from the innocence, hopefulness, and resilience of his young patients. Wellons masterfully intertwines these heartfelt stories with his own experiences of navigating the profound yet haunting realm between life and death.
The stories in this book serve as a testament to the power of human connection and the indomitable spirit of those facing immense challenges at such a young age. I think there’s a lot that can be learned from the unwavering dedication of doctors like Wellons, as well as the perseverance of these patients and their families.
As someone who never considered a career in medicine, I found the book particularly /moving/ – reading about the immense effort, dedication, and compassion of doctors like Wellons made me reflect on the direction I want to take my own career. However, I must admit that I wasn't entirely engrossed in the prose. The book took me a lot longer to finish than anticipated, which could be attributed to unfamiliarity with the medical terminology, being distracted by school, or perhaps the prose itself. I also wished there was a bit more of a narrative theme between the chapters, or from the beginning to the end, although I loved the thoughtfulness of the epilogue. Nevertheless – a beautiful, heartfelt exploration of grief, healing, and the strength that lies within each of us.

books.title: The Radium Girls: The Dark Story of America’s Shining Women
books.author: Kate  Moore
books.review:
This was such an entrancing journey across 20th century American history – beginning in the lead-up to World War I, drifting through the somber shades of the Great Depression, and skimming the onset of World War II. Moore adeptly captures the profound impact these epochs had on a group of women, whose narratives had, for too long, lingered in obscurity.

The book stands as a stark chronicle, charting the complex intersection of law, medicine, worker's rights, and women’s rights – especially as feminism was still very much in its relative first-wave infancy. Moore illuminates the stark contrasts and discrepancies between state laws, laying bare the differing legal battles the radium girls waged depending on their geography. The story powerfully highlights the women's unwavering resilience and tenacity, set against the backdrop of America's dynamic shifts in legal, social, and medical arenas.

Yet, despite its gripping thematic tapestry across an incredibly fascinating time, the book’s prose is severely lacking. Moore's tendency to hop between locations and characters disperses the narrative's cohesion, often clouding the heartfelt tales of these brave women. I couldn't help but be jarred by excessively sprawling sentences, such as:

Having studied these long-term effects of radiation, the CHR scientists – who had, at first, been looking for a magic threshold of radiation exposure, under which no harm was done – ultimately came to agree with Martland, who had warned decades before that “the normal radioactivity of the human body should not be increased.”

Such grossly verbose phrasing (50+ word sentences the length of paragraphs?), riddled with an overabundance of dashes and commas, disrupt an otherwise immersive reading experience. Moreover, glaring oversights like misrepresenting Dr. Flinn's academic credentials – mislabeling his PhD as a doctorate in philosophy (yikes on a bike) – raise eyebrows, indicating lapses in editorial oversight. Lines like:

Flinn was not a medical doctor. His degree was in philosophy. He was, as the Consumers League put it, “a fraud of frauds.”

serve as a stark reminder of the need for meticulous editing, since this immediately seemed odd to me as a lay reader. The writing and style are a solid 2, maybe a 2.5 in my opinion… But the overall story was quite excellent, and I really liked it.

While Radium Girls' narrative brilliance shines bright, its stylistic choices certainly dim its luster. Nonetheless, it remains a poignant reminder of a dark period in American history, and the indomitable spirit of the women who sought justice against incredible odds. It resurrects the overlooked stories of women facing dire adversities and stands as a testament to their enduring spirit. The book also sheds light on the ever-evolving nexus of law (& comparative law), health and medicine, and human rights, reminding us of the battles that paved the way for contemporary liberties: rights many take for granted today.

books.title: The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks
books.author: Rebecca Skloot
books.review: Very compelling love/kind-of-dislike read. This was such a tragic and infuriating read on so many fronts -- 1) how the family was initially, unintentionally exploited by scientists // 2) the question of our bodily autonomy wrt consent in scientific/research settings // 3) race (& gender) in the Jim Crow era // 4) Skloot's somewhat questionable journalistic integrity re: portrayal of suffering and questionable consent wrt to Lacks family // 5) profit distribution after this book was published, as well as when the HBO movie came out. I was definitely left yearning for more insight as to how the family really benefited from Skloot's narrative, because articles I've read have left me with more questions than answers about when this family will stop being exploited... Nevertheless, Skloot does an excellent job at delving deep into HeLa's profound scientific impact while simultaneously addressing the raw, emotional stories of the lives behind the cells and raising the moral quandaries of tissue ownership and consent.

books.title: Shoe Dog: A Memoir by the Creator of Nike
books.author: Phil Knight
books.review:
I always considered “Bad Blood” to be the apex of non-fiction business biographies and was surprised to see how the reviews for this book were even more positive. Shoe Dog is a fantastic, riveting read. I never thought that a business book in an industry I harbor no particular interest or feeling towards would move me to tears. I think I peaked in life already…

Shoe Dog intricately weaves the incredible journey of the founder of Nike, one of the world’s most recognizable brands. Knight’s writing style is engaging and articulate, and it’s clear that he’s well-rounded -- he's an excellent writer who immaculately yields literary devices to enhance an already impressive story, highlighting the tenacity and perseverance that he and his team exhibited in the early days of starting the company.

What truly sets this book apart from other business books I’ve read is its focus on international business. The book begins with his yearlong backpacking journey around the globe in 1960s politically sensitive post WWII / Vietnam War era. His ability to navigate cultural differences, negotiate deals, and build relationships across the world in a pre-Google Translate, pre-Internet and search era is hard to fathom today. He excellently synthesizes building a business from (almost scratch) to political tensions to personal lessons on the power of motivation and perseverance, and dealing with grief.

Buuuuuutt…
This book still leaves a lot of room for frustration, and I realize a lot of this is from the bias of reading it in the 21st century. Knight comes across as very privileged, elitist, and insufferable at times, and this shows in his writing, which he plays off as “his management style”. His parents paid for college, his Stanford MBA, and his year-long trip around the world in the early era of commercialized flights.
What I found especially frustrating was how his wife, Penny, was a key <10 early employee of Nike, until she got married… And until she had kids... Then it became another story about how a bunch of white men who were lucky and worked hard did business together and had their wives take care of the kids at home.
It’s pretty much impossible to have both an incredible work and family life, and Knight does touch on this as an irreversible regret, but I found it exasperating to read how this very key and capable woman’s /plotline/ became almost relegated to being a stay-at-home-wife so Phil can still have his sons and company. It’s a lot easier to have family and a kids if someone else is taking care of them. But I’m sure he knows this; it just felt exasperating to read as a young woman in business.

Overall, an excellent read.

books.title: American Prometheus: The Triumph and Tragedy of J. Robert Oppenheimer
books.author: Kai Bird
books.review:
'American Prometheus' is an absolute epic of a tale, unfolding the intricate life of J. Robert Oppenheimer with unparalleled meticulousness, crafting a narrative so rich and exhaustive that it sometimes risks obscuring the central storyline with its deluge of details.

The intertwining of politics, law, ethics, and the social climate with what seems like a fundamentally apolitical and deeply technical event -- the inception of the atomic bomb -- is fascinatingly depicted.
It brings to the forefront the interplay of disparate forces shaping such a seminal event in history. The political interactions extend into personal politics as well, showcasing not only the macro influences on Oppenheimer's life, but also the delicate web of individual relationships and rivalries, like the (pretty insane) drama with Lewis Strauss. It's also interesting to think about how the broader political landscapes beyond the World War, like the McCarthy Era & the assassination of JFK, all play major influences as well.

That said, despite its thoroughness, the book sometimes felt very narrow and assumes a fairly high level of background knowledge from its readers. I guess the assumption makes sense, given the expected target audience of someone who'd willingly choose to delve into 700+ pages on this topic, but I found myself needing to search for more background context on some of the technical details & political timeline, which definitely interrupted the flow of reading.

Oppenheimer was by all counts a genius, but I appreciated how despite his privilege and lofty intellectual standing, the book does not shy away from portraying his flaws, family struggles, and ethical dilemmas, rendering a much more humanizing and relatable image of the man. It lays bare the human behind the legend, allowing us mere mortals to empathize with his internal and external battles.

This is an absolutely compelling read, rich in detail and perspective, albeit a bit overwhelming in its granularity at times. Nevertheless, for those who actually want to understand who tf most of those humans were in the movie, this book is excellent.

books.description: Over a year on the New York Times bestseller list and more than a million copies sold. The essential universe, from our most celebrated and beloved astrophysicist. What is the nature of space and time? How do we fit within the universe? How does the universe fit within us? There’s no better guide through these mind-expanding questions than acclaimed astrophysicist and best-selling author Neil deGrasse Tyson. But today, few of us have time to contemplate the cosmos. books.title: The Sisters of Auschwitz
books.author: Roxane van Iperen
books.description: WINNER of the Opzij Literature Prize 2019! During the Second World War two Jewish sisters - Janny and Lien Brilleslijper - run one of the largest hideaways in The Netherlands: The High Nest, a villa in The Gooi area. While the last remaining Jews are being hunted in The Netherlands, the lives of dozens of hideaways kept going for better or for worse, right under the noses of their National Socialist neighbours.
